accept: act update --claim in a repo with no upstream succeeds with exit 0 (rebase is short-circuited or treated as success when no upstream exists).
When --claim finds the issue already assigned to the calling node, treats it as a no-op success rather than 'lost race'.
Test added for both: (a) claim against repo without upstream, (b) idempotent re-claim by the same node.

If --isolated is the intended escape hatch for local-only repos, that is documented in act help workflow as the canonical pattern, AND --claim still doesn't write the op when the rebase will fail without it.
payload.description:
Discovered 2026-05-10 by UX-eval agent in /tmp/act-ux-eval (no remote, fresh git init). Sequence:

  $ act update --claim act-3cdc
  claim: pull --rebase: gitops: no upstream remote configured
  exit 1

But act show afterwards reveals status=in_progress and assignee was set. The claim op was written; only the post-write rebase failed. Worse: a retry of the same --claim then prints 'Lost claim race for act-3cdc (winner=4397b575)' — it lost the race against itself.

A fresh agent reading the first error reasonably concludes 'claim failed, retry,' and the retry's misleading 'lost race' error makes them think a different agent grabbed it. Two agents could end up wedged on this. --isolated should not be required to make local-only repos work; the canonical 7-step loop in CLAUDE.md does not mention it. Showstopper for any local-first or new-repo workflow.
payload.title: act update --claim broken when no upstream remote configured
